text: --- PAGE 1 --- FTX’s $950 Million Bankruptcy Fees Among Costliest Since Lehman Lack of records, corporate controls sent adviser fees higher Most creditors poised to get back 118% of what they were owed Sam Bankman-Fried Photographer: Yuki Iwamura/Bloomberg By Jonathan Randles --- PAGE 2 --- February 26, 2025 at 2:17 PM GMT The cost of FTX’s bankruptcy is approaching $1 billion, cementing the implosion of Sam Bankman-Fried’s crypto enterprise as one of costliest Chapter 11 cases in US history. Nearly $948 million was paid to more than a dozen firms hired to work on the bankruptcy through Jan. 2, and more than $952 million in fees have been approved by the court, records show. Hefty as it is, that outlay appears to be paying off for the crypto platform’s creditors. Most customers are poised to get back 118% of what they were owed, a rare outcome in the Chapter 11 process, where creditors usually end up getting pennies on the dollar. The debtholders can thank the throngs of lawyers and financial advisers that have tracked down billions of dollars in digital assets and cash scattered across a byzantine network of accounts, legal experts told Bloomberg News. The beneficiaries include hedge funds that picked up FTX customer claims that traded as low as 10 cents on the dollar after the company collapsed. FTX, which sought protection in November 2022, said last week that it had commenced initial customer distributions. Lawyers are still sifting through a number of disorganized legal entities under the FTX umbrella, seeking more assets to give to creditors. Some lawsuits related to the fallout remain outstanding, --- PAGE 3 --- including a complaint against Binance Holdings Ltd. seeking nearly $1.8 billion. FTX's Road to a $950 Million Bankruptcy The Chapter 11 case has racked up big fees for FTX's primary advisers. [Each bar is on 1st of each month: Paid Cumulative 1.12.24 $947.8M Alvarez & Marsal 1.12.24 $306.1M Sullivan & Cromwell 1.12.24 $248.6M John Ray 1.12.24 $8.1M ($562.8M for the last three)] --- PAGE 4 --- Source: Bankruptcy court records Note: Figures reflect fees paid through January 2. Court records indicate that the high-profile bankruptcies of crypto players Celsius Network LLC, BlockFi Inc., Genesis Global and Voyager Digital Holdings Inc. cost about $502 million combined. The ongoing FTX case is nearly double that. FTX’s lead law firm, Sullivan & Cromwell LLP, has been paid more than $248.6 million and financial adviser Alvarez & Marsal has been paid roughly $306 million, according to court records. Advisers representing the interests of FTX customers and other creditors have charged roughly $110.3 million in fees. It’s likely the most expensive Chapter 11 case of the past several years, according to fee data provided by BankruptcyData. The eye-watering cost of FTX’s repayment plan, approved in October, “would have been cheaper” if the company “had bothered to have books and records, but they didn’t,” said Harvard Law School professor Jared Ellias, who has studied the costs of large Chapter 11 cases. John Ray, the restructuring adviser who was appointed FTX’s chief executive officer when it collapsed, said when the crypto firm filed bankruptcy that he had never seen “such a complete failure of corporate controls and such a complete absence of trustworthy financial information” in his 40-year career, which includes guiding Enron through Chapter 11. Ray’s consulting firm has been paid more than $8 million, according to court records. --- PAGE 5 --- Sullivan & Cromwell and FTX declined to comment. Alvarez & Marsal didn’t respond to a request for comment. FTX's Bankruptcy Fees Are Among The Costliest Since Lehman Play Play 1:09 WATCH: The cost of FTX’s bankruptcy is approaching $1 billion, making it one of the costliest bankruptcies since Lehman Brothers collapsed in 2008. The high cost of FTX’s bankruptcy is coming amid a steady increase in the cost of legal and other professional services, Ellias said. The daily cost of bankruptcy appears to have increased significantly between 2010 and 2022, and, on average, Chapter 11 fees consumed more of a corporate debtor’s pre-bankruptcy assets, according to research he published this month. Katherine Stadler, a lawyer who was appointed to independently review FTX’s fees, said in a 2023 report that the case was extraordinary because of “the largely unregulated financial system in which the Debtors (and other similar financial technology companies) operate,” the global scale of the operation and a lack of even basic corporate governance. Though Stadler’s report said that the bankruptcy was on track to be “very expensive by any measure,” she added that the professionals’ performance was also remarkable. FTX Fees Dwarf Other Crypto Bankruptcies --- PAGE 6 --- FTX's bankruptcy is nearly double the costs incurred by Celsius, Genesis, BlockFi and Voyager, combined. Source: Bankruptcy records [Celsius $229.0M, Genesis $131.9M, BlockFi $81.1M, Younger $60.1M] The bankruptcy of

case was extraordinary because of “the largely unregulated financial system in which the Debtors (and other similar financial technology companies) operate,” the global scale of the operation and a lack of even basic corporate governance. Though Stadler’s report said that the bankruptcy was on track to be “very expensive by any measure,” she added that the professionals’ performance was also remarkable. FTX Fees Dwarf Other Crypto Bankruptcies --- PAGE 6 --- FTX's bankruptcy is nearly double the costs incurred by Celsius, Genesis, BlockFi and Voyager, combined. Source: Bankruptcy records [Celsius $229.0M, Genesis $131.9M, BlockFi $81.1M, Younger $60.1M] The bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers remains the most expensive Chapter 11 case in history, costing nearly $6 billion, according to a 2019 report by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. The cost of restructuring Puerto Rico’s public debt has surpassed $2 billion, Bloomberg News reported earlier this month, making it the most expensive municipal bankruptcy in US history. Both Ray and James Bromley, a Sullivan & Cromwell attorney, also oversaw the bankruptcy of telecommunications giant Nortel Networks, which cost more than $2 billion. More recently, drugmakers have racked up substantial fees in opioid bankruptcies, including Purdue Pharma LP, which has paid more than $939 million after filing Chapter 11 in September 2019, according to court papers. --- PAGE 7 --- — With assistance from Steven Church Follow all new stories by Jonathan Randles
